verdict: All three posts and the newsletter are approved. The batch achieves what the best VRM Sales Library content does: it translates a framework into practitioner behavior without losing the framework's precision. The listing appointment is treated as the real unit of analysis, not as a metaphor. Scripts are specific enough to use. Field notes add something the body copy doesn't already say.
strengths: Post 1 correctly identifies the hero/guide distinction as a diagnostic for why operators lose listing appointments, and translates it into a concrete behavior change (open with questions, not credentials) rather than just restating Miller's concept

Post 2 introduces the three-level problem framework through an owner-churn lens, which is the right VRM application: the six-months-later churn that has no clear cause. The diagnostic questions are usable in a real appointment.
Post 3 correctly pairs the clear plan with the transitional CTA, giving operators a complete close sequence rather than just one tactic. The owner-type callout for Delegators is precise.
The newsletter builds a full annotated listing appointment arc that the individual posts couldn't support. The owner-type variation section at the end is the highest-value extension beyond the posts.
No em dashes present. No exclamation points in body copy. Soft quantifiers used appropriately throughout. CTA footers are consistent and intact.
problems: Post 1's field note is the weakest of the three. It addresses the Skeptic correctly but is slightly general in its claim. Could be sharpened to make the Skeptic's specific fear (being burned again) more explicit rather than just 'defenses up'.

The newsletter's NEXT WEEK section names the next book as The Lost Art of Closing by Anthony Iannarino, which may not be accurate given the current book queue. Verify against BOOK_HITLIST.md before publishing.
required_fixes: Verify the NEXT WEEK book reference in the newsletter against the current posting schedule before publishing.
